fields.Text:
Middle-aged patient with painless epistaxis and recurrent nasal obstruction. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Inverted papilloma}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q1. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. The imaging demonstrates a mass (red arrows) located in the lateral nasal wall with focal coarse calcifications, as well as bony resorption and extension into the maxillary sinus.




Open review Q1

fields.Text: Inverted papilloma is a benign sinonasal tumor classically associated with {{c1::painless epistaxis/nasal obstruction, focal calcifications, bony remodeling, and male predominance}}.
fields.Extra:
Q1. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. The imaging demonstrates a mass (red arrows) located in the lateral nasal wall with focal coarse calcifications, as well as bony resorption and extension into the maxillary sinus.






Open review Q1

fields.Text: Inverted papilloma is seen more often in {{c1::males}}, not females.
fields.Extra:
Q1. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. The imaging demonstrates a mass (red arrows) located in the lateral nasal wall with focal coarse calcifications, as well as bony resorption and extension into the maxillary sinus.

fields.Text:
Acute back pain with leg pain and tingling. Disc abnormality shown?

{{c1::Disc extrusion}}
fields.Extra:
Q2. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Terminology in reporting spinal imaging is important, as specific terms provide specific information.





Open review Q2

fields.Text: A disc extrusion has herniated disc material with a neck/base that is {{c1::narrower than the displaced fragment}}.
fields.Extra:
Q2. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Terminology in reporting spinal imaging is important, as specific terms provide specific information.

fields.Text:
Gamma camera image of a bar phantom. What does this test measure?

{{c1::Spatial resolution and spatial linearity}}
fields.Extra:
Q3. Incorrect; difficulty: moderate. The image shows a bar phantom, which is used to measure both spatial resolution and spatial linearity.




Open review Q3

fields.Text: A gamma-camera bar phantom is used to assess {{c1::spatial resolution and spatial linearity}}.
fields.Extra:
Q3. Incorrect; difficulty: moderate. The image shows a bar phantom, which is used to measure both spatial resolution and spatial linearity.





Open review Q3

fields.Text: A bar phantom does not only test field uniformity; it assesses {{c1::spatial resolution and spatial linearity}}.
fields.Extra:
Q3. Incorrect; difficulty: moderate. The image shows a bar phantom, which is used to measure both spatial resolution and spatial linearity.

fields.Text:
Young patient with chest pain and continuous diaphragm sign. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Pneumomediastinum}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q4. Correct; difficulty: easy. There is gas in the soft tissue of the lower neck (red arrows), and there is a continuous diaphragm sign, indicating air in the mediastinum.






Open review Q4

fields.Text: Pneumomediastinum can produce the {{c1::continuous diaphragm sign}} on chest radiograph.
fields.Extra:
Q4. Correct; difficulty: easy. There is gas in the soft tissue of the lower neck (red arrows), and there is a continuous diaphragm sign, indicating air in the mediastinum.








Open review Q4

fields.Text:
Femur radiograph with lateral cortical stress-type abnormality. Medication history to ask about?

{{c1::Long-term bisphosphonate use}}
fields.Extra:
Q5. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Long-term (> 5 years) bisphosphonate use can lead to atypical femoral fractures, usually seen in the lateral cortex of the diaphysis, as in this case. Upon detection of atypical femoral fracture, bisphosphonate should be discontinued.





Open review Q5

fields.Text: Long-term bisphosphonate use can cause {{c1::atypical femoral fractures along the lateral femoral cortex}}.
fields.Extra:
Q5. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Long-term (> 5 years) bisphosphonate use can lead to atypical femoral fractures, usually seen in the lateral cortex of the diaphysis, as in this case. Upon detection of atypical femoral fracture, bisphosphonate should be discontinued.






Open review Q5

fields.Text:
Slow-growing solid subungual great-toe mass in a child. Expected radiographic finding?

{{c1::Well-circumscribed radio-opacity}}
fields.Extra:
Q6. Correct; difficulty: hard. This picture is characteristic of a subungual exostosis, a benign osteocartilaginous tumor of the distal phalanx, frequently occurring beneath the nail bed.





Open review Q6

fields.Text: Subungual exostosis appears radiographically as a {{c1::well-circumscribed radio-opaque lesion arising from the distal phalanx}}.
fields.Extra:
Q6. Correct; difficulty: hard. This picture is characteristic of a subungual exostosis, a benign osteocartilaginous tumor of the distal phalanx, frequently occurring beneath the nail bed.

fields.Text:
Young patient with progressive dyspnea and thickened nondilated LV on cardiac MRI. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q7. Correct; difficulty: easy. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is an autosomal dominant genetic cardiac disease that results in marked hypertrophy of the myocardium leading to a nondilated, thickened left ventricle (red arrows) seen here on these long axis (bottom image) and short-axis (top image) T2 weighted cardiac MRIs.






Open review Q7

fields.Text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is an {{c1::autosomal dominant}} disease with marked myocardial hypertrophy and a nondilated LV.
fields.Extra:
Q7. Correct; difficulty: easy. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is an autosomal dominant genetic cardiac disease that results in marked hypertrophy of the myocardium leading to a nondilated, thickened left ventricle (red arrows) seen here on these long axis (bottom image) and short-axis (top image) T2 weighted cardiac MRIs.








Open review Q7

fields.Text:
Empty radiopharmaceutical container with radioactive-material label. What must be done before unrestricted disposal?

{{c1::Remove or deface radioactive labels}}
fields.Extra:
Q8. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Adequate information must be available to workers to enable them to handle radioactive materials safely, minimize exposure, and ensure that the proper material is administered to all patients.




Open review Q8

fields.Text: Before disposing of an empty radiopharmaceutical container in unrestricted trash, the worker must {{c1::remove or deface radioactive-material labels}}.
fields.Extra:
Q8. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Adequate information must be available to workers to enable them to handle radioactive materials safely, minimize exposure, and ensure that the proper material is administered to all patients.





Open review Q8

fields.Text: An empty radioactive-material container can enter unrestricted disposal only after {{c1::radioactive labels are removed or defaced}}.
fields.Extra:
Q8. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Adequate information must be available to workers to enable them to handle radioactive materials safely, minimize exposure, and ensure that the proper material is administered to all patients.





Open review Q8

fields.Text:
Comatose trauma patient with DWI/GRE brain findings. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Diffuse axonal injury}}

1/4
fields.Extra:
Q9. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Diffuse axonal injury shows characteristic microhemorrhages at gray-white matter junctions on GRE and SWI sequences. These hemorrhagic lesions appear as hypointense foci in corpus callosum, brainstem, and subcortical white matter. SWI is most sensitive for detecting these paramagnetic blood products.




Open review Q9

fields.Text: Diffuse axonal injury shows {{c1::microhemorrhages at gray-white junctions, corpus callosum, brainstem, and subcortical white matter}} on GRE/SWI.
fields.Extra:
Q9. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Diffuse axonal injury shows characteristic microhemorrhages at gray-white matter junctions on GRE and SWI sequences. These hemorrhagic lesions appear as hypointense foci in corpus callosum, brainstem, and subcortical white matter. SWI is most sensitive for detecting these paramagnetic blood products.








Open review Q9

fields.Text: Declining an unnecessary duplicate test reflects commitment to {{c1::a just distribution of finite resources}}.
fields.Extra:
Q10. Correct; difficulty: moderate. The radiologist’s attempt to prevent an unnecessary and superfluous test signifies their commitment to a just distribution of finite resources.




Open review Q10

fields.Text: Meckel diverticulum is a remnant of the {{c1::vitelline/omphalomesenteric duct}}.
fields.Extra:
Q11. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Meckel's diverticulum represents persistence of the ileal end of the omphalomesenteric duct, which explains its characteristic antimesenteric location and separate vascular supply—features that distinguish it from acquired diverticulosis and help in imaging diagnosis.




Open review Q11

fields.Text: Other than sacrum, the most common chordoma site is the {{c1::skull base/clivus}}.
fields.Extra:
Q12. Correct; difficulty: easy. Approximately 85% of all chordomas occur within the skull base/clivus or sacrum, with ~50% arising from the sacrum and ~35% arising from the skull base/clivus.




Open review Q12

fields.Text:
Incidental adrenal lesion containing macroscopic fat. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Myelolipoma}}
fields.Extra:
Q13. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Myelolipoma is a fatty lesion of the adrenal gland that can be suggested by the presence of macroscopic fat, i.e., fat density on CT examination, high signal on T1 weighted images, or loss of signal on fat-suppressed imaging.





Open review Q13

fields.Text: Adrenal myelolipoma is suggested by {{c1::macroscopic fat}} in an adrenal lesion.
fields.Extra:
Q13. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Myelolipoma is a fatty lesion of the adrenal gland that can be suggested by the presence of macroscopic fat, i.e., fat density on CT examination, high signal on T1 weighted images, or loss of signal on fat-suppressed imaging.






Open review Q13

fields.Text: In NSCLC staging, a 4.8-cm tumor with multiple ipsilateral mediastinal/subcarinal nodal stations is {{c1::T2bN2bM0}}.
fields.Extra:
Q14. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The 9th edition of the International Association for the Study of Lung Cancer TNM staging criteria for non-small cell lung cancer subdivides N2 into N2a (single ipsilateral mediastinal/subcarinal station) versus N2b (multiple ipsilateral mediastinal/subcarinal stations). This change emphasizes counting the number of affected lymph node stations rather than total number of lymph nodes involved. The N2b designation generally results in higher stage classifications compared to N2a, reflecting worse prognosis with multi-station involvement and impacting treatment decisions including surgical respectability.







Open review Q14

fields.Text:
Child with large heterogeneous cerebral hemispheric mass. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Supratentorial ependymoma}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q15. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Supratentorial ependymomas are predominantly extraventricular, arising within cerebral hemisphere parenchyma. They appear more heterogeneous than infratentorial ependymomas with greater tendency for cyst formation, calcifications, and hemorrhage. They are typically large at presentation with mixed enhancement patterns.







Open review Q15

fields.Text: Supratentorial ependymomas are often {{c1::extraventricular cerebral hemispheric masses}} with heterogeneous cystic/calcific/hemorrhagic components.
fields.Extra:
Q15. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Supratentorial ependymomas are predominantly extraventricular, arising within cerebral hemisphere parenchyma. They appear more heterogeneous than infratentorial ependymomas with greater tendency for cyst formation, calcifications, and hemorrhage. They are typically large at presentation with mixed enhancement patterns.










Open review Q15

fields.Text:
Contrast extravasation shown. Patient factor that increases complication severity?

{{c1::Inability to communicate symptoms}}
fields.Extra:
Q16. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: The inability of patients to communicate signs and symptoms related to contrast extravasation are associated with increased volume of extravasated contrast and higher risk of compartment syndrome.




Open review Q16

fields.Text: Contrast extravasation complications are more severe when the patient is {{c1::unable to communicate symptoms}}.
fields.Extra:
Q16. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: The inability of patients to communicate signs and symptoms related to contrast extravasation are associated with increased volume of extravasated contrast and higher risk of compartment syndrome.





Open review Q16

fields.Text:
Chest pain with normal ECG/echo and coronary CTA abnormality. Symptom etiology?

{{c1::Inter-arterial RCA course}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q17. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: An inter-arterial course of the RCA is an uncommon anatomic variant thought to be associated with an increased risk of cardiac ischemia. It is sometimes known as the “malignant course of the right coronary artery.”




Open review Q17

fields.Text: An inter-arterial RCA course is the {{c1::malignant RCA course}} associated with increased ischemic risk.
fields.Extra:
Q17. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: An inter-arterial course of the RCA is an uncommon anatomic variant thought to be associated with an increased risk of cardiac ischemia. It is sometimes known as the “malignant course of the right coronary artery.”






Open review Q17

fields.Text:
Dilated coronary sinus with left-sided mediastinal venous structure. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Persistent left superior vena cava}}
fields.Extra:
Q18. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Persistent left superior vena cava (PLSVC) appears as a tubular vascular structure coursing along the left side of the mediastinum from the junction of left subclavian and internal jugular veins, traveling adjacent to the aortic arch, and typically draining into the right atrium via the coronary sinus. The pathognomonic imaging finding is coronary sinus dilatation without evidence of right heart congestion, which serves as the key radiologic clue suggesting PLSVC presence on cross-sectional imaging.




Open review Q18

fields.Text: Persistent left SVC is suggested by a left mediastinal venous structure with {{c1::coronary sinus dilation without right-heart congestion}}.
fields.Extra:
Q18. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Persistent left superior vena cava (PLSVC) appears as a tubular vascular structure coursing along the left side of the mediastinum from the junction of left subclavian and internal jugular veins, traveling adjacent to the aortic arch, and typically draining into the right atrium via the coronary sinus. The pathognomonic imaging finding is coronary sinus dilatation without evidence of right heart congestion, which serves as the key radiologic clue suggesting PLSVC presence on cross-sectional imaging.





Open review Q18

fields.Text: Working effectively as a team member or leader falls under {{c1::interpersonal and communication skills}}.
fields.Extra:
Q19. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Interpersonal and Communication Skills is the core competency requiring physicians to work effectively both as team members and as leaders when needed.




Open review Q19

fields.Text:
Screening mammogram requiring additional diagnostic workup. BI-RADS category?

{{c1::BI-RADS 0}}
fields.Extra:
Q20. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: BI-RADS 0 indicates an incomplete study requiring additional imaging evaluation before final assessment, typically assigned at screening mammography when findings need diagnostic workup.





Open review Q20

fields.Text: BI-RADS 0 means {{c1::incomplete assessment requiring additional imaging evaluation}}.
fields.Extra:
Q20. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: BI-RADS 0 indicates an incomplete study requiring additional imaging evaluation before final assessment, typically assigned at screening mammography when findings need diagnostic workup.

fields.Text:
MR enterography with multiple arrowed small-bowel outpouchings and no Crohn complications. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Small bowel diverticulosis}}
fields.Extra:
Q21. Correct; difficulty: hard. The images show numerous diverticula arising from the small bowel (red arrows).





Open review Q21

fields.Text: Multiple small-bowel outpouchings on enterography without skip lesions, strictures, abscess, or fistula suggest {{c1::small bowel diverticulosis}}.
fields.Extra:
Q21. Correct; difficulty: hard. The images show numerous diverticula arising from the small bowel (red arrows).






Open review Q21

fields.Text:
Carotid plaque ultrasound concept. What risk-score limitation matters?

{{c1::Framingham risk may not correlate with plaque burden or events}}
fields.Extra:
Q22. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Carotid plaque appears as echogenic, hypoechoic, or heterogeneous deposits causing luminal narrowing on ultrasound, with hypoechoic plaques being more unstable. Framingham Risk Score limitations include not incorporating carotid plaque burden or morphology that ultrasound can detect for improved cardiovascular risk stratification.





Open review Q22

fields.Text: Framingham Risk Score may miss risk because it does not incorporate {{c1::carotid plaque burden or morphology}}.
fields.Extra:
Q22. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Carotid plaque appears as echogenic, hypoechoic, or heterogeneous deposits causing luminal narrowing on ultrasound, with hypoechoic plaques being more unstable. Framingham Risk Score limitations include not incorporating carotid plaque burden or morphology that ultrasound can detect for improved cardiovascular risk stratification.






Open review Q22

fields.Text: Ebstein anomaly involves the {{c1::tricuspid valve}} and right ventricle.
fields.Extra:
Q23. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Ebstein's anomaly involves congenital abnormality of the tricuspid valve and right ventricle.




Open review Q23

fields.Text:
Altered mental status with hyperdense basilar artery sign. Next study?

{{c1::CTA}}
fields.Extra:
Q24. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: The hyperdense basilar artery sign on unenhanced CT indicates basilar thrombosis with high specificity and predicts poor outcomes. Urgent CTA is indicated to confirm occlusion and guide endovascular intervention.




Open review Q24

fields.Text: The hyperdense basilar artery sign on noncontrast CT should be followed urgently by {{c1::CTA}}.
fields.Extra:
Q24. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: The hyperdense basilar artery sign on unenhanced CT indicates basilar thrombosis with high specificity and predicts poor outcomes. Urgent CTA is indicated to confirm occlusion and guide endovascular intervention.





Open review Q24

fields.Text:
Chronic fever, dyspnea, cough with peripheral pulmonary opacities. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q25. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia shows characteristic peripheral and upper lobe consolidation and ground-glass opacities on CT, described as the photographic negative of pulmonary edema - this peripheral distribution pattern is key to distinguishing it from other pneumonias and helps guide steroid therapy.




Open review Q25

fields.Text: Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia classically causes {{c1::peripheral/upper-lobe migratory airspace disease}}.
fields.Extra:
Q25. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia shows characteristic peripheral and upper lobe consolidation and ground-glass opacities on CT, described as the photographic negative of pulmonary edema - this peripheral distribution pattern is key to distinguishing it from other pneumonias and helps guide steroid therapy.






Open review Q25

fields.Text: Solitary pulmonary nodule calcification patterns concerning for malignancy include {{c1::amorphous, reticular, or punctate calcification}}.
fields.Extra:
Q26. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Amorphous, reticular, and punctate calcification patterns in lung nodules are associated with malignancy, particularly mucinous adenocarcinoma, contrasting with classic benign calcification patterns (central, laminar, popcorn, diffuse). These irregular calcification patterns should raise suspicion for lung cancer rather than provide reassurance of benignancy.




Open review Q26

fields.Text: Gamma-camera spatial resolution quality control is assessed with a {{c1::bar phantom}}.
fields.Extra:
Q27. Correct; difficulty: easy. Spatial resolution is the ability of a system to accurately distinguish two closely spaced objects as two separate entities.




Open review Q27

fields.Text: Bladder cancer on ultrasound appears as {{c1::fixed vascular wall thickening or intraluminal mass}}.
fields.Extra:
Q28. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: On ultrasound, bladder cancer appears as iso/hypoechoic wall thickening with possible intraluminal extension and vascularity on color Doppler. The presence of vascularity distinguishes it from non-vascular clots, stones and debris. Real-time assessment on ultrasound has the added benefit of differentiating mobile clots/stones from fixed tumors.




Open review Q28

fields.Text: The major determinant of intrinsic spatial resolution in PET is {{c1::detector face width/size}}.
fields.Extra:
Q29. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The major determinant of intrinsic spatial resolution in a PET scanner is the size of the detector face.




Open review Q29

fields.Text: PET intrinsic spatial resolution is primarily determined by {{c1::detector face width}}, not coincidence timing window.
fields.Extra:
Q29. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The major determinant of intrinsic spatial resolution in a PET scanner is the size of the detector face.




Open review Q29

fields.Text:
Premenopausal breast MRI scheduling. Traditional recommended menstrual-cycle window?

{{c1::Days 7-14 after onset of menses}}
fields.Extra:
Q30. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Historical practice restricted breast MRI to menstrual cycle week 2 based on studies showing lowest parenchymal enhancement, but current evidence demonstrates no diagnostic performance differences across cycle phases, supporting flexible scheduling due to improved MRI technology and interpretation.





Open review Q30

fields.Text: Traditional breast MRI scheduling in premenopausal patients targets {{c1::days 7-14 after menses onset}}.
fields.Extra:
Q30. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Historical practice restricted breast MRI to menstrual cycle week 2 based on studies showing lowest parenchymal enhancement, but current evidence demonstrates no diagnostic performance differences across cycle phases, supporting flexible scheduling due to improved MRI technology and interpretation.

fields.Text:
CT with diffuse calcification of the gallbladder wall. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Porcelain gallbladder}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q31. Correct; difficulty: easy. Explanation: Axial and coronal post-contrast CT images demonstrate diffuse thick calcification of the entire gallbladder wall.





Open review Q31

fields.Text: Diffuse calcification of the gallbladder wall is {{c1::porcelain gallbladder}}.
fields.Extra:
Q31. Correct; difficulty: easy. Explanation: Axial and coronal post-contrast CT images demonstrate diffuse thick calcification of the entire gallbladder wall.

fields.Text:
Cerebellar tigroid lesion on MRI. Associated syndrome?

{{c1::Cowden syndrome}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q32. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: The tigroid appearance on cerebellar MRI is virtually diagnostic of Lhermitte-Duclos Disease, which automatically places the patient in the major criteria category for Cowden syndrome diagnosis and triggers comprehensive cancer surveillance protocols given the significantly elevated lifetime malignancy risks in this population.

fields.Text: Lhermitte-Duclos disease with a cerebellar tigroid MRI appearance is associated with {{c1::Cowden syndrome}}.

fields.Text:
Elderly patient with RUQ pain, fever, vomiting, gastric distention, and gallstone obstruction. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Bouveret syndrome}}
fields.Extra:
Q33. Correct; difficulty: hard. Two coronal and an axial CT at the level of the stomach and duodenum show that the stomach is grossly distended (red arrow).





Open review Q33

fields.Text: Bouveret syndrome is {{c1::gastric outlet obstruction from an ectopic gallstone}}.
fields.Extra:
Q33. Correct; difficulty: hard. Two coronal and an axial CT at the level of the stomach and duodenum show that the stomach is grossly distended (red arrow).

fields.Text:
MRCP with dorsal duct drainage via the minor papilla. Variant?

{{c1::Pancreas divisum}}
fields.Extra:
Q34. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Pancreas divisum on MRCP shows predominant pancreatic duct drainage into the minor papilla superior to the bile duct level, with absent or minimal communication between dorsal and ventral ducts.




Open review Q34

fields.Text: Pancreas divisum shows predominant dorsal duct drainage through the {{c1::minor papilla}}.
fields.Extra:

fields.Text: Subchondral insufficiency fracture of the knee most commonly affects {{c1::postmenopausal women}}.
fields.Extra:
Q35. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Subchondral insufficiency fracture of the knee most commonly affects women over 50 years of age. Low bone mineral density associated with menopause contributes to increased risk.




Open review Q35

fields.Text:
Severe persistent bleeding after multiple D&Cs with early draining uterine vascular nidus. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Uterine AVM/arteriovenous fistula}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q36. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is the gold standard for diagnosing uterine arteriovenous malformation. DSA best demonstrates the characteristic vascular nidus with direct arteriovenous communication. This case shows early arterial filling of the nidus with rapid venous drainage without intervening capillary beds.






Open review Q36

fields.Text: Uterine AVM/fistula on DSA shows {{c1::early arterial filling of a nidus with rapid venous drainage}}.
fields.Extra:
Q36. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is the gold standard for diagnosing uterine arteriovenous malformation. DSA best demonstrates the characteristic vascular nidus with direct arteriovenous communication. This case shows early arterial filling of the nidus with rapid venous drainage without intervening capillary beds.

fields.Text:
Post-glioma therapy MRI/perfusion follow-up. Appropriate management?

{{c1::Continued follow-up}}
fields.Extra:
Q37. Correct; difficulty: easy. An important component of brain tumor imaging is the use of gadolinium perfusion magnetic resonance images (MRI).





Open review Q37

fields.Text: In treated glioma, perfusion MRI helps distinguish treatment effect from recurrence; low-suspicion findings may warrant {{c1::continued follow-up}}.
fields.Extra:
Q37. Correct; difficulty: easy. An important component of brain tumor imaging is the use of gadolinium perfusion magnetic resonance images (MRI).

fields.Text:
Transient small bowel intussusception with spontaneous resolution. Follow-up test?

{{c1::Capsule endoscopy}}
fields.Extra:
Q38. Correct; difficulty: hard. The CT images show a small bowel intussusception (green arrows), which contains mesenteric fat and vasculature.





Open review Q38

fields.Text: After spontaneous resolution of small-bowel intussusception, {{c1::capsule endoscopy}} can evaluate for a lead point or mucosal lesion.
fields.Extra:
Q38. Correct; difficulty: hard. The CT images show a small bowel intussusception (green arrows), which contains mesenteric fat and vasculature.

fields.Text:
Older man with dysuria and trabeculated bladder with diverticulum. Underlying process?

{{c1::Bladder outlet obstruction}}
fields.Extra:
Q39. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Large bladder diverticula in older men indicate acquired bladder outlet obstruction requiring urological evaluation. These carry 0.8% to 10% malignancy risk and necessitate cystoscopy, urine cytology, and treatment of underlying obstruction to prevent complications including infection, stones, and cancer.




Open review Q39

fields.Text: Large bladder diverticula with trabeculation in older men suggest {{c1::acquired bladder outlet obstruction}}.
fields.Extra:
Q39. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Large bladder diverticula in older men indicate acquired bladder outlet obstruction requiring urological evaluation. These carry 0.8% to 10% malignancy risk and necessitate cystoscopy, urine cytology, and treatment of underlying obstruction to prevent complications including infection, stones, and cancer.





Open review Q39

fields.Text:
Chest CT with nodules centered in the secondary pulmonary lobule core. Distribution?

{{c1::Centrilobular}}
fields.Extra:
Q40. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Pulmonary nodules are a common finding and are usually incidental. They can be classified according to size, morphology, or distribution.




Open review Q40

fields.Text: Centrilobular nodules are centered around the {{c1::bronchioles/pulmonary arterioles in the core of the secondary pulmonary lobule}}.
fields.Extra:
Q40. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Pulmonary nodules are a common finding and are usually incidental. They can be classified according to size, morphology, or distribution.

fields.Text: Fluoroscopic spatial resolution is affected by {{c1::focal spot size, receptor characteristics, magnification mode, patient motion, and geometry}}.
fields.Extra:
Q41. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Fluoroscopic resolution is determined by several factors: focal spot size, image receptor characteristics (pixel size, phosphor thickness), magnification mode, patient motion, and geometric factors - with magnification mode and focal spot being the primary controllable determinants during imaging.




Open review Q41

fields.Text:
Thoracic spine MRI with cord/conus compression by soft tissue mass. Next step?

{{c1::Hospital admission for treatment of cord compression}}
fields.Extra:
Q42. Correct; difficulty: easy. The images demonstrate a soft tissue mass resulting in convex deformity of a lower thoracic vertebral body posteriorly, and severe impingement of the spinal cord and conus.




Open review Q42

fields.Text: Imaging evidence of spinal cord compression requires {{c1::hospital admission/emergent treatment}}.
fields.Extra:
Q42. Correct; difficulty: easy. The images demonstrate a soft tissue mass resulting in convex deformity of a lower thoracic vertebral body posteriorly, and severe impingement of the spinal cord and conus.

fields.Text:
Stereotactic biopsy diagram with needle displaced horizontally from target. Error type?

{{c1::X-axis error}}
fields.Extra:
Q43. Correct; difficulty: hard. Pre-fire and post-fire -15 degree and +15 degree images are acquired during stereotactic biopsy.




Open review Q43

fields.Text: On stereotactic biopsy post-fire images, horizontal needle-target displacement represents {{c1::X-axis error}}.
fields.Extra:
Q43. Correct; difficulty: hard. Pre-fire and post-fire -15 degree and +15 degree images are acquired during stereotactic biopsy.

fields.Text:
High-speed MVC with descending thoracic aortic injury on CT. Next step?

{{c1::Emergent surgical or IR referral}}
fields.Extra:
Q44. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: In the setting of traumatic aortic injury, CT can show direct signs (intramural hematoma, intimal flap, pseudoaneurysm) and indirect signs (periaortic hematoma, caliber change). For descending aortic TAI post-MVC, endovascular repair is preferred first-line treatment with lower mortality and reduced complications compared to open repair.





Open review Q44

fields.Text: Descending traumatic aortic injury after MVC should prompt {{c1::emergent surgical/interventional radiology referral, usually for endovascular repair}}.
fields.Extra:
Q44. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: In the setting of traumatic aortic injury, CT can show direct signs (intramural hematoma, intimal flap, pseudoaneurysm) and indirect signs (periaortic hematoma, caliber change). For descending aortic TAI post-MVC, endovascular repair is preferred first-line treatment with lower mortality and reduced complications compared to open repair.






Open review Q44

fields.Text:
Cirrhotic patient with jaundice and vascular invasion but no discrete liver mass. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Infiltrative hepatocellular carcinoma}}
fields.Extra:
Q45. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Infiltrative hepatocellular carcinoma in cirrhosis can be radiographically occult with no discrete mass visible, mimicking confluent fibrosis - look for vascular invasion as a key diagnostic clue since this subtype has high propensity for venous involvement.





Open review Q45

fields.Text: Infiltrative HCC in cirrhosis may be occult as a discrete mass; a key clue is {{c1::vascular invasion}}.

fields.Text:
BRCA patient breast MRI with high T2/STIR nonenhancing thin-walled lesions. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Simple cysts}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q46. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Simple breast cysts appear as thin-walled structures with high T2/STIR signal and no enhancement on contrast-enhanced sequences. These benign lesions are the most common breast finding, require no follow-up, and are distinguished from malignancy by their lack of enhancement.




Open review Q46

fields.Text: Simple breast cysts are {{c1::thin-walled, high T2/STIR, and nonenhancing}}.

These benign lesions are the most common breast finding, require no follow-up, and are distinguished from malignancy by their lack of enhancement.

fields.Text:
Older male with tender subareolar breast tissue on mammogram/US. Next step?

{{c1::Follow-up with the referring clinician}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q47. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Gynecomastia commonly causes tender subareolar fibroglandular tissue in older men. Classic mammographic patterns (fan-shaped or flame-shaped subareolar tissue) combined with tenderness are diagnostic.




Open review Q47

fields.Text: Classic tender subareolar gynecomastia in an older man requires {{c1::clinical/referring-provider follow-up rather than biopsy}}.

fields.Text:
Cervical imaging with well-corticated ossicle above hypoplastic odontoid. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Os odontoideum}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q48. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Os odontoideum is a well-corticated ossicle superior to a hypoplastic odontoid with a large gap between fragments. This entity is distinguished from Type II dens fracture by smooth corticated margins and larger separation. Can be orthotopic (normal position) or dystopic (ectopic).





Open review Q48

fields.Text: Os odontoideum is distinguished from type II dens fracture by {{c1::smooth corticated margins and a larger gap}}.

This entity is distinguished from Type II dens fracture by smooth corticated margins and larger separation. Can be orthotopic (normal position) or dystopic (ectopic).

fields.Text: Quality control is reactive error detection, quality assurance is monitoring/prevention, and quality improvement is {{c1::systematic performance enhancement}}.
fields.Extra:
Q49. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Quality Control (QC) reactively measures and corrects errors/poor quality when found, Quality Assurance (QA) maintains consistent performance through monitoring and prevention strategies, and Quality Improvement (QI) proactively implements systematic changes to enhance performance beyond current levels.




Open review Q49

fields.Text:
Cystic thickened endometrium on imaging. Treatment association?

{{c1::Tamoxifen for breast cancer}}
fields.Extra:
Q50. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Tamoxifen treatment can result in distinct cystic thickening of the endometrium. This can be seen in up to 50% of patients undergoing breast cancer treatment with tamoxifen.




Open review Q50

fields.Text: Tamoxifen therapy for breast cancer can cause {{c1::cystic endometrial thickening}}.
fields.Extra:

fields.Text:
Lateral medullary infarct symptoms with dysphagia, ataxia, crossed sensory deficits. Eponym?

{{c1::Wallenberg syndrome}}
fields.Extra:
Q51. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Lateral medullary syndrome shows restricted diffusion in the lateral medulla from posterior inferior cerebellar artery infarction with ipsilateral Horner syndrome, ataxia, and crossed hemisensory deficits. Vertebral artery dissection occurs in up to one-quarter of cases requiring neurovascular imaging.





Open review Q51

fields.Text: Wallenberg syndrome is {{c1::lateral medullary syndrome from PICA/vertebral artery territory infarction}}.
fields.Extra:
Q51. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Lateral medullary syndrome shows restricted diffusion in the lateral medulla from posterior inferior cerebellar artery infarction with ipsilateral Horner syndrome, ataxia, and crossed hemisensory deficits. Vertebral artery dissection occurs in up to one-quarter of cases requiring neurovascular imaging.






Open review Q51

fields.Text:
Post-thyroidectomy I-131 uptake in medullary thyroid cancer with neck star artifact. Diagnosis/artifact?

{{c1::Residual thyroid tissue; star artifact}}
fields.Extra:
Q52. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from parafollicular C-cells that lack sodium-iodide symporter expression and therefore do not concentrate radioiodine. Any I-131 uptake post-thyroidectomy is most compatible with residual normal thyroid tissue, not medullary thyroid carcinoma recurrence. A star artifact is common when using a low- or medium-energy collimator during image acquisition; use of high-energy collimators can significantly reduce this artifact).




Open review Q52

fields.Text:
Radiograph with flowing candle-wax cortical hyperostosis. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Melorheostosis}}
fields.Extra:
Q53. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Melorheostosis shows characteristic candle-wax cortical hyperostosis in a unilateral segmental distribution following sclerotome patterns, representing a mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia affecting both endochondral and intramembranous ossification.

fields.Text: Melorheostosis produces {{c1::flowing candle-wax cortical hyperostosis}} in a unilateral segmental/sclerotomal distribution.

fields.Text:
Tc-99m DTPA renal study. Function measured?

{{c1::Glomerular filtration rate}}
fields.Extra:
Q54. Correct; difficulty: easy. DTPA (diethylene triamine pentaacetic acid) is filtered by the glomeruli and is neither appreciably secreted nor reabsorbed by the tubular system.




Open review Q54

fields.Text: Tc-99m DTPA evaluates {{c1::glomerular filtration rate}} because it is filtered without appreciable tubular secretion or reabsorption.

fields.Text:
Infant with multiple fractures, normal care context, and family history of fractures. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Osteogenesis imperfecta}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q55. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Osteogenesis imperfecta can be confused with child abuse. Radiographic and physical features specific to OI, as well as a positive family history, can help establish the proper diagnosis.







Open review Q55
